topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor’s cheerful pronouncements about the 'character' of the old Victorian house grated on Sarah’s nerves. Every mention of the property's history—the whispered tales of shadowy figures and sudden disappearances—only sharpened the tightness in her chest. She traced the faded floral wallpaper in the drawing-room, her fingers lingering on a spot where a dark stain seemed stubbornly embedded in the plaster. *They* had lived here, the stories went. People who had done things she couldn't even fathom. She watched the agent point out the antique fireplace, a piece of heavy, dark wood that felt strangely significant under her touch. A familiar, hollow ache settled beneath her ribs. She imagined what kind of secrets resided behind those walls, secrets that offered a kind of notoriety she desperately craved. She forced a bright smile, a performance she knew didn't reach her eyes, and tried to focus on the pristine condition of the kitchen, a space utterly devoid of any lingering shadows.

[story 2]

Mark stood by the window, the afternoon sun catching the dust motes dancing in the air. He kept glancing toward the darkened hallway, a compulsion pulling at him. The house was undeniably beautiful—high ceilings, original hardwood floors—but it felt tainted, heavy with unspoken narratives. He ran a hand over the molding, feeling the cool, rough texture, a stark contrast to the smooth, modern finishes of his own apartment across town. He remembered the local news reports, the way the police had cordoned off the property years ago, the hushed tones of the investigators. A strange, hot flush crept up his neck. He needed something, something substantial, something that carried weight. He started pacing the living room, his steps quickening, his jaw tightening. He needed to feel connected to something dramatic, something that had truly *happened*, not just settled into quiet domesticity.

[story 3]

“It’s got real bones, doesn’t it?” the inspector remarked, tapping a section of the basement wall with a gloved finger. Liam nodded, but his gaze was fixed on the faint, almost invisible scuff marks near the foundation. He felt a distinct tightening in his shoulders, a sudden need to possess something more compelling than his own carefully curated life. He found himself studying the architecture with an intensity that bordered on obsession. He pictured the kind of attention such a place would command—the kind of recognition that comes from being intrinsically linked to a significant event. A low, almost involuntary sigh escaped him. He wanted the narrative, the undeniable proof that his surroundings held a deeper, more tumultuous past than his own quiet existence allowed.
